name: Al Foster
abstract: Aloysius Tyrone Foster (January 18, 1943 – May 28, 2025) was an American jazz drummer. Foster's professional career began in the mid-1960s, when he played and recorded with hard bop and swing musicians including Blue Mitchell and Illinois Jacquet.

about_quotes.speaker: Buster Williams
about_quotes.evidence: I did that album and I wrote out a majority of the music for it, Herbie Hancock was on it, Wayne Shorter, Al Foster, trumpet player named Shunzo Ohno.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buster Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Diblasio
about_quotes.evidence: We took a train with McCoy and Al Foster and Eddie Gomez. And we were riding on the train with him to that spot we were playing in Japan

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Allen
about_quotes.evidence: I recorded one just about a month before that with Kenny Barron and Al Foster and George Mraz.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Allen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 13, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Liebman
about_quotes.evidence: I didn’t know anybody except Al in the band. I know Al. At least I knew Al Foster.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Liebman — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...I have Buster Williams and Tony, Eddie Gomez and Al Foster... [INTERVIEWEE] Maybe I overstated the case... I can't think of any others.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: I think it was Al Foster on the drums, I think Al was playing drums. ... And Al Foster on drums. ... And Al played that calypso really cool
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
